paragraphs.text: 2005-2025 nétcomm 20 ANNI DI RIVOLUZIONE DIGITALE

Netcomm is the Italian Consortium for Digital Commerce, a reference point in the field of e-commerce and digital transformation on the national and international scene, with over 480 members, national, international and small-medium enterprises of Made in Italy excellence operating along the entire supply chain of the digital sector.

Overall, Netcomm welcomes the European Commission's initiative aimed at addressing the challenges of the digital economy with the ambitious goal of harmonizing the European regulatory framework, making it more suitable for the competitive environment, strengthening user protection and contributing to the construction of an increasingly secure, transparent and trustworthy digital environment.

The assessment presented in the 2024 Fitness Check raises a number of issues and dynamics in business models that are problematic for consumers and digital users and concludes that there are some gaps in the current legislative framework.

In our view, several of the issues identified in the Fitness Check are certainly legitimate and need attention, further study and appropriate action. However, at the same time, we do not agree with the conclusion that additional legislation is needed. The current EU legal framework is sufficient to effectively address emerging issues and to provide enforcement authorities with appropriate and effective tools. In support of this position, we refer to the study presented by Professors Kühling and Sauerborn of the University of Regensburg, which addresses many of the concerns previously identified and further argues that any legislative intervention should be sectoral and proportionate. In recent years, the European Commission has introduced a wide range of legislative measures aimed at strengthening consumer protection and ensuring a level playing field for businesses within the Digital Single Market. However, the rapid proliferation of such initiatives has raised growing concerns regarding the sustainability of the regulatory framework, the consistency of the rules and the implementation capacity of the Member States.
In Netcomm's view, it is important to focus on some aspects.

evidence_quotes.quote: divergences in the timing and manner of national transposition

paragraphs.text: First, the regulatory fragmentation has become a structural problem: divergences in the timing and manner of national transposition led to legal uncertainty and undermine regulatory predictability.
Netcomm
Via Serbelloni, 2 – 20122 Milan www.consorzionetcomm.it
Organisational model pursuant to Legislative Decree 231/2001 - Code of Ethics

evidence_quotes.quote: the overlap between consumer protection legislation and digital regulation which often apply concurrently to the same commercial practices.

paragraphs.text: Second, the overlap between consumer protection legislation and digital regulation which often apply concurrently to the same commercial practices. This regulatory layering increases the risk of inconsistency and leads to the duplication of burdens for economic operators.

Notably, these two challenges (fragmentation and overlap) represent a significant obstacle especially for Italian SMEs. The Italian entrepreneurial fabric is mainly made up of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, which face considerable difficulties in to maintain regulatory compliance. Ensuring compliance entails: i) being aware of the applicable rules, ii) understanding how to apply them – a particularly critical point, given that small business and operators often lack in-house legal expertise and cannot consistently rely on external legal; and iii) implementing the necessary adjustments, which frequently requires investment in technical and IT compliance measures – another source of financial strain.

Finally, the difficult in enforcing the existing rules. This has a double consequence: on the one hand, the rules appear to be "ineffective" and, on the other hand, ineffective enforcement create to competitive asymmetries, as compliance is effectively ensured only by the more diligent and responsible operators. In light of these concerns, the proposed Digital Fairness Act appears disproportionate to the actual scope of the issues identified and risks increasing the critical issues set out previously (fragmentation, overlap and enforcement).

It would therefore be advisable to introduce a pause in the legislative process for new rules, combined with greater attention to the effective implementation of existing legal framework. Where necessary, softer, and more proportionate instruments should be preferred to avoid further complexity and ensure legal consistency across the Single Market.

One of the issues addressed in the Fitness Check concerns, for instance, the case-by-case approach. We are aware that this approach poses challenges for authorities in terms of its practical application; however, it remains one of the most effective tools in consumer law for addressing the emerging issues in this domain. Its inherent flexibility is exactly what makes the UCPD a future-proof tool/instrument. In any case, the Directive could be improved, which could benefit both businesses and enforcement bodies.

evidence_quotes.quote: review the applicable terms and conditions ((such as withdrawal procedures or how to access guarantees).

paragraphs.text: Another point we wish to raise concerns the assertion that consumers are generally more vulnerable online. There are undoubtedly critical aspects and the most vulnerable categories of consumers that require protection and as mentioned above, we welcome the willingness to address the gaps and critical issues. However, it is necessary to consider that the consumers in the online environment have access to much more complete information than they would find when shopping offline in physical stores, where for instance, they do not be able to review the applicable terms and conditions ((such as withdrawal procedures or how to access guarantees).

A key area for meaningful intervention would be to invest seriously on consumer education, an issue we will return to in the following sections. A final important consideration is that many of the relevant legislative instruments – which will be referred to at the proper time, such as DSA, DMA, AI Act – are still in the implemented phase. Therefore, we believe it is premature at this stage to assess and evaluate their effectiveness, as they have not yet had the time and opportunity to express their full potential.
Having outlined these general considerations, we now proceed with a more targeted analysis of the topics subject to consultation.

evidence_quotes.quote: the notion of deceptive models (or dark patterns)

paragraphs.text: In general, it should be noted that the notion of deceptive models (or dark patterns) can take various forms. In fact, there is no clear consensus on what constitutes a "dark model," making new legislation potentially problematic. The term is often applied too broadly, sometimes encompassing legitimate business practices. Therefore, it is crucial to distinguish between genuinely harmful practices (which are already illegal under existing legislation) and widely used and lawful business activities.

It should be noted that EU law already provides a list of such practices, as well as the applicable legal provisions; consequently, the existing instruments already provide a sufficient legal basis to address these concerns, making the introduction of further layers of regulation unnecessary.

evidence_quotes.quote: Deceptive design is already addressed under the Digital Services Act (DSA)

paragraphs.text: Deceptive design is already addressed under the Digital Services Act (DSA), and **we support a harmonised approach that clarifies how this framework interacts with other legislative instruments** such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), and the Artificial Intelligence Act.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns as practices that "materially, intentionally or de facto, distort or compromise the ability of service recipients to make autonomous and informed choices or decisions"
the design of online interfaces that deceive or manipulate users or otherwise impair their ability to make free and informed decisions.

paragraphs.text: Recital 67 of the DSA offers a functional definition, describing dark patterns as practices that "materially, intentionally or de facto, distort or compromise the ability of service recipients to make autonomous and informed choices or decisions". This definition should be adopted as a regulatory standard, supported by updated or newly issued guidelines. In addition, Article 25 of the DSA prohibits the design of online interfaces that deceive or manipulate users or otherwise impair their ability to make free and informed decisions.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading if its overall presentation is likely to mislead the average consumer

paragraphs.text: As regards the UCPD, its technology-neutral and principle-based framework allows it to address both existing and emerging commercial practices, demonstrating that regulators already have the necessary tools in place to protect consumers, while maintaining the necessary flexibility to address evolving practices. Article 6(1) of the UCPD considers a commercial practice to be misleading if its overall presentation is likely to mislead the average consumer and lead to transactional decisions that he would not have taken otherwise, even if the information is technically accurate.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading design
subliminal, manipulative, or deceptive techniques

paragraphs.text: Other instruments under EU law also address misleading design; Article 5(1) of the Artificial Intelligence Act prohibits AI systems that use subliminal, manipulative, or deceptive techniques with the aim or effect of materially distorting user's behavior and impairing decision-making capacity. Similar concerns are addressed under the GDPR framework.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns **are not exclusive to e-commerce**; such manipulative practices also occur offline:

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, we would like to point out that dark patterns **are not exclusive to e-commerce**; such manipulative practices also occur offline:

evidence_quotes.quote: use limited-time promotions to artificially create urgency.
strategically placing essential goods at the back of shops to encourage impulse purchases.
design product packaging in a way that misrepresents the actual content and/or quality.

paragraphs.text: i. use limited-time promotions to artificially create urgency. ii. strategically placing essential goods at the back of shops to encourage impulse purchases. iii. design product packaging in a way that misrepresents the actual content and/or quality.
In light of this awareness, our contribution:

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: **Q1: Regarding dark patterns, do you think new EU actions need to be taken to improve consumer protection and the functioning of the single market?**

evidence_quotes.quote: any further initiative aimed to regulate Dark Patterns would be inappropriate.

paragraphs.text: In our view, any further initiative aimed to regulate Dark Patterns would be inappropriate. Any future regulatory proposal should be strictly targeted at addressing demonstrable legal gaps. The focus should remain on the effective and consistent enforcement of existing legislation; any action should be limited to harmonising definitions or clarifying the scope of the existing rules.
Q4: Describe the EU-specific actions (non-regulatory measures) you support in relation to the dark patterns above?

Firstly, action is needed to increase consumer awareness, greater awareness means greater ability to identify unfair practices. In addition, we encourage the Commission to promote guidelines on harmful business practices, developed in cooperation with experts and stakeholders. Such guidance should clearly distinguish between harmful behaviour and legitimate business models, considering the complex trade-offs involved in user interfaces.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Q5: Please describe the specific EU actions (on enforcement) you support with respect to the above indicated dark patterns?

a. Clarification of enforcement rules. The Digital Fairness Act should clarify and reduce "legal grey areas". While courts and public authorities have already established principles and rules for many commercial practices, these remain poorly recognised or lack harmonisation across the EU. Article 12 of the UCPD offers a case-by-case approach and partially reverses the burden of proof. It is a key provision within the principled-base framework, enabling an important degree or flexibility while requiring traders to self-assess the fairness of their practices by exercising "maximum effort." The implications of these requirements vary depending to the size of the company, the market share and the nature of the activities or goods/services offered. However, the operation of this article and its practical implications for traders would benefit from further clarification. Self-evaluation is often challenging, if not unfeasible for some actors, due to: (i) lack of awareness of the obligation; (ii) lack of tools to conduct assessments and (iii) difficulties engaging in dialogue with public authorities.

evidence_quotes.quote: the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network should be restructured into an entity that provides opinions, guidance and harmonises interpretations of the Directive.
harmonises interpretations of the Directive.

paragraphs.text: b. Strengthen cooperation at European level. For instance, the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network should be restructured into an entity that provides opinions, guidance and harmonises interpretations of the Directive.

evidence_quotes.quote: major enforcement challenges, such as limited resources,
enforcement authorities underutilise these mechanisms.

paragraphs.text: c. More effective enforcement of existing rules. We believe that the existing consumer protection framework is effective in combating unfair commercial practices despite it should be implemented with better enforcement (for instance, under the Omnibus Directive). It seems that currently, enforcement authorities underutilise these mechanisms. Simply adding new layers of regulation without addressing major enforcement challenges, such as limited resources, would likely increase complexity without improving consumer protection.
SECTION 2: ADDICTIVE DESIGN.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading or persuasive design
addictive design

paragraphs.text: Online interfaces are often the main point of interaction between businesses and consumers; when assessing misleading or persuasive design, it is essential to clearly distinguish between legitimate commercial practices and unlawful manipulation. Regarding addictive design, we believe that current EU legislation already addresses the main concerns, including those listed in the consultation. In particular, the following provisions are relevant:
Article 34 of the Digital Services Act requires very large online platforms to conduct annual systemic risk assessments, including risks to users' mental well-being.

Article 28(1) of the DSA requires proportionate measures to ensure a high level of privacy and protection of minors, while the guidelines under the child protection framework include principles to enhance online protection, risk assessments, and the methodologies for implementing the age protection mechanism.

evidence_quotes.quote: exploit vulnerabilities related to age, disability, or socioeconomic conditions in such a way as to cause significant harm.

paragraphs.text: Article 5(2) of the AI Act prohibits the use of AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities related to age, disability, or socioeconomic conditions in such a way as to cause significant harm.

evidence_quotes.quote: exploits known consumer vulnerabilities of consumers (e.g. gambling behaviour, lack of impulse control)

paragraphs.text: The provisions of the UCPD may also apply where a trader exploits known consumer vulnerabilities of consumers (e.g. gambling behaviour, lack of impulse control) to unduly influence purchasing decisions.
Within the limits of the existing legal framework, providers should maintain the ability to determine how to structure such interactions.

Before introducing new rules, it would be important to carry out further investigations to identify specific, clear, and documented practices, characteristics or gaps. In this regard, the Commission's initiative to launch an EU-wide inquiry into digital well-being is a positive step, as are industry-led efforts to provide customisable tools, in particular for minors, across services and devices.

Therefore, the DFA should: (i) address and identified gaps, (ii) adopt a flexible risk-based approach, (iii) avoid broad and one-size-fits-all measures that may not be suitable for all contexts and, (iv) most importantly, avoid overly prescriptive or descriptive requirements.
In light to the above considerations, we can therefore state that any new legislation can only be supported if it aims to clarify the scope of the existing framework. Specifically, refer to:
**Question 4. Additional or more specific actions you support:**

evidence_quotes.quote: Addictive designs go beyond the traditional merchant-consumer dynamic. Their main goal is not to influence purchasing decisions, but to maximize user engagement and time spent on an interface.

paragraphs.text: Addictive designs go beyond the traditional merchant-consumer dynamic. Their main goal is not to influence purchasing decisions, but to maximize user engagement and time spent on an interface. Interfaces cater to website users, regardless of whether the interface has a commercial purpose. This issue is more related to ethical considerations in interface design than marketing or sales practices. It falls outside the scope of the UCPD and cannot be effectively addressed through the lens of commercial practices.
**SECTION 3 – SPECIFIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL PRODUCTS, SUCH AS IN VIDEO GAMES**

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: Some features of digital products, especially video games, have raised concerns from a consumer protection perspective. The current EU legislative framework provides the necessary tools to address these issues:

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading practices

paragraphs.text: The UCPD includes provisions on misleading practices and specifically addresses the characteristics of gambling (including in the guidelines published by the Commission).
The Consumer Rights Directive sets out pre- and post-contractual information requirements that cover in-app purchases made with real money, including those made using vouchers or gift cards.

evidence_quotes.quote: uncertainty-based mechanisms that resemble gambling

paragraphs.text: National legislation may also regulate specific aspects, in particular in relation to uncertainty-based mechanisms that resemble gambling.

evidence_quotes.quote: adopt a technology-neutral and risk-based approach;
promoting effective transparency regarding potential risks;
identify specific risks rather than specific business models or design features;

items.topic: Exemptions For Micro And Small Enterprises

evidence_quotes.quote: leverage industry best practices, many of which have already been implemented voluntarily.

paragraphs.text: In this context, the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) should: (i) adopt a technology-neutral and risk-based approach; (ii) avoid duplication or conflict with existing legislation; (iii) focus on educating and empowering consumers to use such products responsibly, while promoting effective transparency regarding potential risks; (iv) identify specific risks rather than specific business models or design features; v) leverage industry best practices, many of which have already been implemented voluntarily.
This approach would ensure that regulatory action remains proportionate and innovation-friendly, while maintaining high standards of consumer protection.
**SECTION 4 – UNFAIR PERSONALIZATION PRACTICES**

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalization plays a crucial role in shaping online content and services, enhancing user experience, improving the relevance of information and advertising, and contributing to the creation of safer and more age-appropriate digital environments.

paragraphs.text: Personalization plays a crucial role in shaping online content and services, enhancing user experience, improving the relevance of information and advertising, and contributing to the creation of safer and more age-appropriate digital environments.
Personalization is a well-established business practice adapted to the digital context.

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers generally value personalisation.

paragraphs.text: Studies show that consumers generally value personalisation. In fact, consumers tend to prefer receiving a personalized offer, including advertising. A tailored proposal, target to specific preferences, is often appreciated

evidence_quotes.quote: From this perspective, there is no intrinsic conflict between personalised advertising and consumer interests.

paragraphs.text: more than generic content which may instead be perceived as intrusive or irrelevant. From this perspective, there is no intrinsic conflict between personalised advertising and consumer interests.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation does not imply coercion

paragraphs.text: It is also important to stress that personalisation does not imply coercion: personalised recommendations or advertisements do not determine user behaviour.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation is a key economic factor.

paragraphs.text: For businesses, especially small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), which account for over than 90% of companies in Europe, personalisation is a key economic factor. It enables more efficient use of limited marketing resources to target audiences who are most likely to be interested in their products. Without personalised advertising tools, SMEs would be at a serious disadvantage compared to larger competitors or third-country platforms. According to estimates, personalised advertising supports hundreds of thousands of jobs and contributes billions of euros to the EU's GDP—an economic value set to increase further with the growing adoption of AI technologies.

evidence_quotes.quote: That said, personalisation also raises important concerns
profiling

paragraphs.text: That said, personalisation also raises important concerns related to data protection and fundamental rights—particularly regarding profiling, manipulation, and potential harm to vulnerable groups, including minors. We fully acknowledge that these are crucial aspects that must be appropriately addressed and effectively managed.

In this regard, we observe that such concerns are already being effectively addressed within the existing robust legal framework, which offers comprehensive protection, including the following instruments:

evidence_quotes.quote: profiling and automated decision-making.

paragraphs.text: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR): Prohibits the collection of data without a valid legal basis, imposes strict rules on sensitive data and vulnerable groups, and regulates profiling and automated decision-making.

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibits personalised advertising based on sensitive data
prohibits advertising aimed at minors
transparency regarding the parameters of ads

paragraphs.text: Digital Services Act (DSA): prohibits personalised advertising based on sensitive data (Article 26(3)) and prohibits advertising aimed at minors (Article 28(2)), while requiring transparency regarding the parameters of ads (Article 26(1)).

evidence_quotes.quote: Requires consent at the GDPR level when gatekeepers use personal data for online advertising (Article 5(2)).

paragraphs.text: Digital Markets Act (DMA): Requires consent at the GDPR level when gatekeepers use personal data for online advertising (Article 5(2)).
Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD): addresses the use of personalised rankings and recommendations in commercial contexts.
Consumer Rights Directive (CRD): imposes obligations relating to the communication of personalised prices.

evidence_quotes.quote: exploiting individual vulnerabilities in ways that could significantly distort behaviour

paragraphs.text: AI Act: prohibits AI systems from exploiting individual vulnerabilities in ways that could significantly distort behaviour and cause harm (Article 5(1)(b)).

evidence_quotes.quote: addressing emerging personalisation related issues.

paragraphs.text: These legal instruments, together with the existing self-regulatory rules and enforcement mechanisms, already constitute a coherent and solid basis for addressing emerging personalisation related issues. There is no need for further horizontal legislation which would create overlapping conflicting, or redundant obligations, including from an enforcement perspective.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation issues are closely linked to data use and privacy

paragraphs.text: Considering this, we strongly recommend that the Digital Fairness Act refrain from introducing new obligations in this area. Personalisation issues are closely linked to data use and privacy and therefore must continue to be addressed, primarily, under the Data Protection law rather than the Consumer Protection legislation. The Commission should avoid adding overlapping regulations that could lead to legal uncertainty, create conflicting obligations or impose disproportionate burdens on businesses, in particular SMEs. Considering the complexity of the legal framework and numerous application issues that arise in practice, we believe a proportionate and market-oriented approach would involve concrete support measures, including:
Providing interpretative guidance and coordination of existing legal frameworks (e.g., GDPR, DSA, DMA) through communications and similar.

evidence_quotes.quote: The issue of personalization affects the most part of companies

paragraphs.text: Supporting SMEs in understanding and complying with the (existing) rules including through dedicated initiatives. The issue of personalization affects the most part of companies, including micro and small-medium sized ones, which aim to retain customer loyalty and promote their offerings to new audiences. However, these businesses often lack the financial resources for legal advice, and the availability of interpretative guidance and similar tools would provide valuable assistance.
Improving coordination and cooperation among authorities responsible for monitoring and enforcement.
Promoting user education and transparency tools to foster awareness, informed use, and better self-protection.
Supporting best practices and innovation that enhance user control and enable responsible personalisation.

evidence_quotes.quote: minors and vulnerable groups

paragraphs.text: Ensuring that additional safeguards for specific categories (e.g., minors and vulnerable groups) are based on clear and objective criteria, rather than vague notions such as "emotional distress" or "negative mental states", which could undermine legal certainty and enforceability.

Any future regulatory initiative must strike the right balance between user protection, digital innovation, and the fundamental freedom to conduct a business. A risk-based, proportionate, and technologically neutral approach should remain the guiding principle.

evidence_quotes.quote: The issue of influencer marketing is relatively new but has achieved a significant impact on consumers

paragraphs.text: The issue of influencer marketing is relatively new but has achieved a significant impact on consumers; accordingly, we value as positive the legislator’s attention to it. At the same time, we note that the existing regulatory framework already addresses the matter effectively through various key laws and regulations:

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: The AVMS Directive (Article 9(1)),

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (Article 7(2)), and

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act (Article 26)

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: These instruments address and prohibit several practices that may be problematic for consumers. In addition, the Commission’s guidance addresses several key issues, clarifying roles and responsibilities and ensuring further transparency.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: Many services already require influencers to disclose advertising or endorsements during the content upload process, which ensures a certain level of transparency.

paragraphs.text: In light of the current global framework, the Digital Fairness Act should avoid duplicating these laws and should instead focus on strengthening enforcement and promoting self-regulation. Many services already require influencers to disclose advertising or endorsements during the content upload process, which ensures a certain level of transparency.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: Rather than introducing new legislation, a more effective approach would include:

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: Practical guidance: develop clearer and more accessible guidelines for influencers and brands.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: Consumer education: promote awareness and collaborate with social media platforms to develop specific guidance for influencer marketing.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: Strengthening enforcement: the Fitness Check found that 44% of respondents consider current enforcement ineffective, suggesting that addressing enforcement gaps should be a priority.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: In conclusion, the current legal framework is already comprehensive and addresses key issues such as advertising disclosure, brand responsibility, and protection of vulnerable groups. The emphasis should therefore be on improving enforcement of the rules, raising consumer awareness, and promoting self-regulation rather than creating new legislation.

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

section_path: Section 5 – Harmful Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized offers.

paragraphs.text: Pricing in the retail sector is a crucial issue. The retail sector is inherently highly competitive, with a wide variety of prices and personalized offers.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: The matter of pricing and promotional claims is already extensively regulated at both EU and national levels.

paragraphs.text: The matter of pricing and promotional claims is already extensively regulated at both EU and national levels. However, this very high level of regulation—combined with divergent national approaches—has led to inconsistent application of the rules across Member States, sometimes even conflicting or, at minimum, non-harmonised. This has created significant complexity for businesses and confusion for consumers, especially in the case of cross-border purchases, where understanding applicable consumer protection rules is not always straightforward.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: For these reasons, we believe that the current legal framework is already broad enough to cover the relevant issues. Attention should now shift to two fundamental priorities:

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: Clarifying and harmonising the regulatory framework, by providing further guidance on how the rules apply to specific cases.

paragraphs.text: i. Clarifying and harmonising the regulatory framework, by providing further guidance on how the rules apply to specific cases.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: ii. Improving enforcement and oversight, ensuring that the rules are applied consistently across all operators, thereby avoiding competitive distortions.

paragraphs.text: ii. Improving enforcement and oversight, ensuring that the rules are applied consistently across all operators, thereby avoiding competitive distortions.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: Therefore, any legislative action should focus on expanding and improving the application scope of existing rules, rather than introducing new ones.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: Price Indication Directive (PID) and the “30-day lowest price” rule.

paragraphs.text: In this context, one key issue that remains highly debated and unresolved is the Price Indication Directive (PID) and the “30-day lowest price” rule. The fragmented application of this provision among EU Member States has created challenges for both consumers and businesses.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: 30-day lowest price requirement
announcement of a price reduction
reference prices

paragraphs.text: The European Commission’s proposal to revise Article 6 of the PID appears to revisit some of the challenges partly introduced by the 30-day lowest price requirement. We strongly recommend legislative clarification that includes: i) clearer definitions of "announcement of a price reduction" to ensure greater clarity in promotional practices; ii) further clarification on reference prices, in order to ensure consistent application, avoid fragmentation, improve consumer transparency, and facilitate business compliance, while still allowing for pricing flexibility.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: As such, we stress the need for a harmonised EU-level reform, rather than ongoing fragmented national approaches.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: Regarding price comparison, we believe that the principle-based approach of the Directive, combined with case-by-case analysis, allows various business models to coexist and function effectively, adapting to continuous market developments. Therefore, we do not fully agree with the thesis that a "grey area" exists as a result of the principles-based approach of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: According to Article 12 of the Directive, any operator conducting price comparisons must be able to "provide proof of the factual accuracy of their claims."

paragraphs.text: In France, the Supreme Court has established clear criteria for a valid reference price: it must be genuine, effective, and representative. These three fundamental principles, established decades ago, align fully with the Directive’s principles-based framework and its case-by-case approach. According to Article 12 of the Directive, any operator conducting price comparisons must be able to "provide proof of the factual accuracy of their claims." In practice, this means they must demonstrate that their reference price meets the criteria set out by the French Supreme Court. If properly implemented, this article could greatly facilitate enforcement of the Directive.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: The criteria established by the French Supreme Court are closely aligned with the statements included in the questionnaire.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair marketing related to pricing

paragraphs.text: Q.1. Concerning unfair marketing related to pricing, do you think that any new EU actions should be taken to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? Yes, improvements are needed to ensure consistency within the current legal framework, strengthen enforcement, and improve the application of existing rules—without the need to introduce new ones.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: cancellation processes, and automatic renewals.

paragraphs.text: The current EU regulatory framework includes a few rules that address the issue of digital contracts and subscriptions. Existing consumer protection rules, including the Consumer Rights Directive, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive and the Unfair Contract Terms Directive, are integrated into comprehensive pre- and post-contractual information requirements and ensure that consumers are informed and that their rights are protected. These regulations already address many of the challenges related to digital contracts, such as transparency, cancellation processes, and automatic renewals.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: In our view, some areas still require further clarification or specific guidance to improve implementation and enforcement. Likewise, enhanced monitoring and enforcement by competent authorities could ensure greater competitive balance.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, two key aspects should be carefully considered to avoid overly prescriptive rules that could create practical difficulties:

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: **Cancellation processes: flexibility with respect to prescriptively**

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: cancelling a subscription should be as simple as subscribing,
considering any obstacles to cancellation as aggressive commercial practices, with an obligation to provide easily accessible and visible tools.

paragraphs.text: We agree on the need to guarantee consumers transparency, clarity, and ease in withdrawing from digitally signed contracts (such as subscriptions) equal to or greater than that used to enter into the contract. However, these requirements are already provided for within the existing regulatory framework: the Consumer Rights Directive highlights that cancelling a subscription should be as simple as subscribing, considering any obstacles to cancellation as aggressive commercial practices, with an obligation to provide easily accessible and visible tools.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: disproportionate conditions for the termination of services

paragraphs.text: The Digital Markets Act (DMA) also requires gatekeepers to refrain from imposing disproportionate conditions for the termination of services on major platforms.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: cancellation link or button
cancellation mechanisms

paragraphs.text: Imposing a universal cancellation link or button may be overly prescriptive by constraining enterprises in designing interfaces. It may also not be suitable for all types of contracts, such as digital goods with immediate access (such as software) or services related to the physical delivery of products; It would therefore be useful to maintain some flexibility for companies to adapt the cancellation mechanisms to the nature of their services, improving enforcement on the application of the rules.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: **Subscription renewals and free trials: Maintaining consumer choice.**

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: The regulatory framework should continue to allow flexibility for companies in how they manage subscription renewals and free trials.
Automatic reminders for renewal should be proportionate, avoiding over-notification that could overwhelm or confuse consumers.
Businesses should maintain the ability to decide how best to communicate with their customers, depending on the subscription model.

paragraphs.text: The regulatory framework should continue to allow flexibility for companies in how they manage subscription renewals and free trials. Automatic reminders for renewal should be proportionate, avoiding over-notification that could overwhelm or confuse consumers. Businesses should maintain the ability to decide how best to communicate with their customers, depending on the subscription model.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: requiring explicit consent for every renewal or conversion of a free trial could impose excessive burdens on both businesses and consumers.

paragraphs.text: Similarly, free trials serve as an important marketing tool, particularly for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) looking to expand their customer base. While these trials help consumers explore services without immediate engagement, requiring explicit consent for every renewal or conversion of a free trial could impose excessive burdens on both businesses and consumers. This could lead to higher prices, limiting access to those offerings, particularly for smaller developers.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: A separate cancellation button is not necessary if companies already provide clear cancellation mechanisms in accordance with existing legal requirements.
If a new regulation were to introduce such a button, it should allow companies the flexibility to adapt it to the nature of their products and user interfaces.

paragraphs.text: A separate cancellation button is not necessary if companies already provide clear cancellation mechanisms in accordance with existing legal requirements. If a new regulation were to introduce such a button, it should allow companies the flexibility to adapt it to the nature of their products and user interfaces. This would ensure that the solution remains practical and scalable for different industries and business models.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

evidence_quotes.quote: to regulate digital contracts and subscriptions.
refine **cancellation** processes,
maintain **consumer choice** for subscriptions
user-friendly subscription services

paragraphs.text: In conclusion, the current legal framework is already comprehensive enough to regulate digital contracts and subscriptions. Rather than adding new layers of regulation, the focus should be on improving **guidance**, ensuring that businesses have clear and flexible rules that allow them to offer user-friendly subscription services to consumers without excessive burdens. Regulatory efforts should aim to refine **cancellation** processes, maintain **consumer choice** for subscriptions and provide adequate protection for consumers, while ensuring that businesses remain competitive and innovative.

section_path: SECTION 6 – UNFAIR MARKETING RELATED TO PRICING

paragraphs.text: **Simplification.** The current European consumer protection regulatory framework has proven effective in safeguarding consumers' interests, with clear laws ensuring access to transparent information and remedies. The increase in digital regulations as a response to the development of technology and new models of relationship between businesses and consumers is creating an intricate regulatory landscape, which risks creating confusion and limiting consumer access to innovative products.

As widely emphasized, rather than introducing new regulations – that risk overlapping or further complicating the existing framework – it is **necessary to focus on simplifying and harmonizing existing laws**. Therefore, the European Commission should concentrate on improving how current rules interact. This approach would not only simplify the regulatory landscape but also improve the applicability and effectiveness of existing regulations, avoiding the creation of an accumulation of laws that do not address current challenges.

evidence_quotes.quote: the lack of uniform application of regulations
the difficulty of ensuring compliance with the rules by some companies European and non-European based.

paragraphs.text: **Enforcement and regulatory tools.** Among the main critical aspects and gaps in consumer protection, which emerged from the above analysis, we recall the lack of uniform application of regulations and the difficulty of ensuring compliance with the rules by some companies European and non-European based.

evidence_quotes.quote: self-regulatory systems
guidelines and codes of conduct

paragraphs.text: These problems can only be solved with **more effective procedural interventions and tools to ensure a more consistent and effective application of the rules by regulatory and supervisory authorities**. Tools such as self-regulatory systems, guidelines and codes of conduct are just a few examples that would be valuable for companies of all sizes.

In this context, the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network could play a crucial role in promoting dialogue and coordination between national authorities, improving the interpretation of laws and reducing the risk of market fragmentation.
**Question 1.** In your view, are there any concrete measures to simplify consumer laws that could reduce the burden for businesses while maintaining the same level of consumer protection?

**Adopt a risk-based approach.** The Commission should adopt a holistic vision that places simplification as a fundamental principle of regulation, aiming to build a coherent regulatory framework that not only harmonizes various laws but also reduces the risk of conflicts between new regulations and those already in force. It is essential that future legislation be clear, predictable, and focused on effective consumer protection without overburdening businesses.

evidence_quotes.quote: repetitive transactions with the same provider (such as in-app purchases)

paragraphs.text: "Reduction of consumer information obligations under the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD) with regard to repetitive transactions with the same provider (such as in-app purchases) and in automated contracts concluded by consumers using a digital assistant (AI)." AI-based shopping assistants offer significant potential to improve consumer experience and simplify purchasing processes, particularly in repetitive or automated transactions like in-app purchases. With the AI agent market expected to grow substantially, this technology presents opportunities for both innovation and economic growth. However, imposing rigid or disproportionate information obligations—such as requiring AI tools to provide full pre-contractual information for every transaction—could undermine usability and stifle innovation. As AI agents become more autonomous and operate across multiple platforms, clear attribution and liability mechanisms will be essential. Enabling third-party services to recognize and manage interactions with AI agents and allowing businesses to set preferences regarding these interactions will foster trust and legal clarity. In this evolving landscape, structured dialogue, monitoring, and the development of best practices and technical standards—rather than new legislative layers—represent the most effective way to balance innovation with consumer protection, while ensuring consistency with the provisions of the AI Act.

section_path: Question 2. As regards the measures deemed appropriate:

evidence_quotes.quote: Ending the current fragmentation of national rules on price reductions for perishable products under the Price Indication Directive

paragraphs.text: "Ending the current fragmentation of national rules on price reductions for perishable products under the Price Indication Directive, by fully exempting all food products regardless of their characteristics." The EC's suggestion seems to confirm that the general approach set out in Article 6a of the Price Indication Directive is challenging and requires further consideration. Our contribution to section 6 provides a high-level perspective on a possible way forward through a targeted revision of the Price Indication Directive.

section_path: Question 2. As regards the measures deemed appropriate:

evidence_quotes.quote: digital product passport (DPP) accessible via QR codes.

paragraphs.text: Product information requirements should be modernized by shifting non-essential information to digital formats, particularly through the digital product passport (DPP) accessible via QR codes. The current obligation to provide safety information in the language of each Member State creates unnecessary burdens, especially for SMEs, without necessarily benefiting consumers who may speak different languages. This digital-first approach would reduce costs while improving consumer access to comprehensive, multilingual product information that can be easily updated, maintaining physical documentation only for crucial safety details.

section_path: Question 4. Do you think that certain types of information should be provided to consumers exclusively in digital form?

paragraphs.text: As outlined in this contribution, there is significant potential to leverage EU simplification and competitiveness measures to address legal fragmentation. All issues raised in the scope of this question already fall within the scope of the existing legislation. The EC may consider a broader consumer strategy as a mechanism to mitigate the risk of further fragmentation at national level.

section_path: Question 7. In your view, in which of the following areas would EU actions reduce the fragmentation of the single market that may currently exist due to divergences (national laws or interpretations by national courts or authorities?)?

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading practices online

paragraphs.text: Transparency and consumer awareness. A key factor in creating a fairer and safer digital market is increased consumer awareness. Despite the current legal protection, the Commission should invest in consumer education. Promoting greater awareness of misleading practices online would increase the demand for more transparent and secure services, encouraging businesses to improve the quality and reliability of their products. In addition, the implementation of the Digital Markets Act (DMA) is essential to ensure that consumers, in particular those using gatekeeper services, can exercise their freedom of choice in competitive digital markets. This would reduce the scope for UTPs, while preserving innovation and diversity of available services.
In conclusion, Netcomm reiterates its appreciation for the initiative, as it highlights important and crucial issues for the safety of online consumers and the fairness of business practices.

However, achieving this objective does not require new legislation, but rather a rationalisation of the existing rules, focusing on simplification and harmonisation, so that regulations are consistent across all Member States.
In summary, Netcomm’s position can be outlined through the following key points:

i. Avoid regulatory overreach, thus preventing further overlap with existing laws or the introduction of new rules covering areas already regulated. New rules should be introduced only where strictly necessary—for example, to fill genuine gaps or to provide harmonised definitions.

ii. Harmonize and simplify existing legislation, clarifying the scope of application of existing rules, using where possible soft law tools and support measures such as interpretative guidelines, codes of conduct and self-regulation capable of resisting technological evolution and changes in the business model.
iii. Introduce new regulations only after proper evaluation, including thorough effectiveness and following adequate risk assessments to verify the impacts.

evidence_quotes.quote: iv. Improve and strengthen the enforcement of existing rules, improving coordination and cooperation between the Commission, Member States and national authorities.

paragraphs.text: iv. Improve and strengthen the enforcement of existing rules, improving coordination and cooperation between the Commission, Member States and national authorities.
v. Supporting consumers to increase their awareness and knowledge on the use of digital.
Thank you for your attention.
Consorzio Netcomm

Netcomm is the Italian Consortium for Digital Commerce, a reference point in the field of e-commerce and digital transformation on the national and international scene. Founded in 2005, the Consortium brings together over 450 companies made up of international companies and small-medium enterprises of excellence to support the development of digital services, the internationalization of Italian companies and ensure a coherent regulatory framework and protection for consumers and web users.

Netcomm is also one of the founding members of Ecommerce Europe, the European e-commerce association based in Brussels that represents e-commerce associations from 21 EU countries and a number of member companies operating in Europe offering their services in multiple markets, including Italy; the collaboration with Ecommerce Europe is an advantage because it allows us to have a faithful and concrete vision of digital commerce in the European framework.
